ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw pork sausage as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for generic processed meat products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw pork sausage link weighs approximately 75 g before cooking, providing around 216 kcal, 11.5 g protein, and 18.6 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Pork sausage, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pork sausage, raw (CR ID: F06110017). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat value of 9.2 g/100g is cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 10165 (Pork sausage, fresh, raw) as a supporting secondary check; HPB value is primary.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — raw pork sausage contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw pork sausage contains only 0.93 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no GI value is assigned to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 75 g serving is effectively 0.0. GI value is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not applicable for this negligible-carbohydrate food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 739 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; processed meat nitrites compound vascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid raw pork sausage; even a single 75 g link delivers approximately 554 mg sodium, approaching the entire recommended daily limit for high-risk individuals, and sodium nitrites in processed sausage independently elevate arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.2 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance; processed red meat is independently associated with increased T2D incidence per ADA guidelines
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid pork sausage because its high saturated fat content impairs insulin sensitivity and habitual processed red meat consumption is associated with a 19–51% increased risk of T2D progression per ADA and WHO evidence reviews; substitute with lean unprocessed poultry or legumes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.2 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (70 mg/100 g), and sodium (739 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL-C and blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid pork sausage; its combination of saturated fat, trans fat, dietary cholesterol, and very high sodium creates a compounded atherogenic and hypertensive burden, and processed meat consumption is classified as a Group 2A carcinogen by IARC with independent CVD risk associations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), high saturated fat, and sodium nitrites in processed meat are strongly pro-inflammatory, activating TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pork sausage entirely, as its trans fat, saturated fat, and nitrite content are among the most pro-inflammatory dietary exposures for RA, with no anti-inflammatory offset present in this food.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork sausage (raw) is a processed meat product made from seasoned ground pork and pork fat, formed into a cylindrical shape and encased in a natural or synthetic casing. In its raw, uncooked state it displays a pale pink colour with a soft, yielding texture. It is a globally common ingredient used in Western breakfasts, barbecues, and cooked dishes across many cultures. Nutritionally, raw pork sausage is energy-dense at 288 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 24.8 g (of which approximately 9.2 g is saturated fat and ~0.2 g is trans fat) and a moderate protein content of 15.4 g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.93 g per 100 g, making glycemic index not applicable. Sodium is critically high at 739 mg per 100 g, placing this food in the high-concern category for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 70 mg per 100 g. From a clinical perspective, pork sausage is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and nitrite content. It is not halal or kosher in standard formulations. It must be cooked thoroughly before consumption to eliminate food safety risks from raw pork.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a raw pork sausage?
How much sodium is in pork sausage?
Is raw pork sausage high in fat?
Is pork sausage suitable for hypertension?
What is the protein content of pork sausage?
Can I eat pork sausage if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06110017) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimates are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central data for raw pork sausage (NDB 10165). The food identity is unambiguous. Source match is exact.
notes: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data for this specific HPB pork sausage raw entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 10165) was consulted as a secondary check but values were not mapped to avoid source mixing without explicit cross-validation. Saturated fat (9.2 g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (10.9 g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.6 g/100g), and trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10165 (Pork sausage, fresh, raw) as the HPB record does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; these are documented as adapted values. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per HPB source, consistent with a meat-based product containing no plant fibre. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating not applicable; this food contains only 0.93 g carbohydrate per 100 g and glycemic index is not meaningful for negligible-carbohydrate foods; gi_category is set to not_applicable and glycemic load per 75 g serving is effectively 0.0. This food is not halal in standard formulations (pork-based); halal status should be verified against specific product labelling. Raw pork sausage must be cooked thoroughly to a safe internal temperature before consumption.

q1_food_identity.notes: Food name 'Pork Sausage, Raw' is precise, canonical, and unambiguous. This is a generic Western processed meat product with no mandatory non-English language requirement under G6 multilingual rules. English identity is fully satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (288.0), protein_g (15.39), fat_g (24.8), and carbohydrate_g (0.93) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (CR ID: F06110017, confidence 0.95). nutrient_source is fully populated with organization, URL, and match type.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB, meat product with no plant fibre), sodium_mg (739.0), saturated_fat_g (9.2 — adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10165, documented in notes), and sugar_g (0.93) are all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 (sentinel value for not-applicable negligible-carbohydrate food) with gi_category 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source is fully populated referencing the University of Sydney GI Database with clear explanation that GI is not applicable for a food with only 0.93 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The numeric value satisfies the validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301776 (pork sausage) is a defensible exact match. FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pork sausages, raw) is a defensible exact match. SNOMED CT is null with a documented gap note explaining limited SNOMED coverage for generic processed meat food items.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,350 characters), covers food identity, preparation, texture, colour, cultural context, full nutritional profile, clinical relevance for hypertension/T2D/CVD/RA, halal status, and food safety note. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium 739 mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, saturated fat and processed meat T2D risk), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern, saturated fat + trans fat + cholesterol + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis is included as an additional condition. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required.

All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — self-check passed. No mandatory non-English aliases are required for this generic Western food category.

Fatty acid fractions are adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10165 with full documentation. GI is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel for not-applicable status on a negligible-carbohydrate food (0.93 g/100g), with gi_category not_applicable and full gi_source documentation. Ontology anchors are defensible (FoodOn + FoodEx2 exact matches). Health context covers all three mandatory conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with strong clinical and contextual content. Alias coverage is consistent between flat array and structured object. No hard gate failures. Final decision: PASS.
